title: Turing Award Winner: NSA, Public Key Cryptography, Crypto Wars | Martin Hellman
guest_role: Turing Award winner, Stanford professor emeritus, co-inventor of public-key cryptography

sections.title: Why his work broke the law
The story behind Diffie Hellman key exchange
Why inventions happen at similar times
What he worked on after cryptography

key_takeaways: The NSA claimed Hellman's published papers violated ITAR (anything cryptographic was an 'implement of war'); after a 1977 NSA letter to the IEEE, Stanford's counsel deemed the interpretation unconstitutional and Hellman presented his students' papers at Cornell himself so Stanford could defend him rather than them.

The first crypto war was over freedom to publish and DES's 56-bit key: Hellman and Diffie estimated a $20M machine could break DES for ~$10,000 per solution with 1975 technology, and NSA wanted the small key because only they had the budget and problem volume to exploit it — a crude trapdoor via superior computing resources.

The second (Clipper Chip key escrow) and third (San Bernardino iPhone) crypto wars recycled the same idea; his 1995 CRISIS committee concluded key escrow was unsolvable and that classified briefings 'filled in details but did not change our fundamental conclusions.'
Strong encryption cannot be given only to good guys — his analogy: if cars had been invented in the classified world, government would oppose civilian cars despite ambulances and commuting.

Every colleague told him he was crazy to compete with the NSA's budget and head start; he argues the best work looks crazy a priori (GPS, multiple Nobel stories) and credits a contrarian identity forged by childhood bullying.

Diffie-Hellman key exchange is the double-locked strongbox: both parties add and remove their own locks, requiring a commutative one-way function — exponentiation in modular arithmetic over a finite field.

Digital signatures (Diffie's public-key cryptosystem concept, first instantiated by RSA) are what GCHQ's claimed earlier invention lacked entirely; signatures are how iPhones verify Apple software updates with only a public key on the device.

Ralph Merkle independently invented public-key distribution as a Berkeley term project that his professor dismissed and CACM rejected for lacking references; Hellman recruited him to Stanford and helped rewrite the paper.

Stanford's patent was drafted by a law school intern and its claims failed to cover RSA — RSA sold for $250M while the Stanford inventors made virtually nothing; Hellman deliberately made peace with RSA because 'friends are better than enemies.'

Since cryptography he has worked on nuclear risk: he estimates a child born today has worse-than-even odds of living out a natural life due to nuclear weapons, and is pushing H.R. 3564 to require more than sole presidential authority to launch.

Advice to his younger self: get famous and rich first, then save the world — the apparent detour was the shortest distance, since reputation and money at 35 gave him leverage to work on what mattered.

notable_quotes.speaker: Martin Hellman
notable_quotes.quote: I'm from the Bronx... I'm a street fighter with my mouth and my mind. And they picked the wrong person, basically. I wasn't going to back down.

notable_quotes.speaker: Martin Hellman
notable_quotes.quote: You cannot make strong encryption and give it only to the good guys and not to the bad guys.

notable_quotes.speaker: Martin Hellman
notable_quotes.quote: All my colleagues, and it's not an exaggeration, told me I was crazy to work in cryptography... And the best ideas often seem crazy ahead of time.

notable_quotes.speaker: Martin Hellman
notable_quotes.quote: We need to save humanity from an infant death. We've only been around about 100,000 years.
